title: Application Cannot Write Logs
scenario: An application log file exists at ~/logs/app.log but the intern user cannot append to it. The file was accidentally created as root during a deployment script, so its ownership is root:root. Even though the file permissions are 644 (owner read-write, group and others read-only), only the owner — root — can write to it. The intern needs to take ownership of the file before the application can log normally.
symptoms: echo 'Application started' >> logs/app.log returns Permission denied
File exists but is owned by root
mission: Investigate why you cannot write to ~/logs/app.log, fix the ownership so that intern owns the file, and then write 'Application started' to it.

hints.title: Inspect file details
hints.content: Run 'ls -l logs/app.log' to see who owns the file and what permissions are set. The third and fourth columns show the owning user and group.

hints.title: Change ownership
hints.content: The 'chown' command changes file ownership. The syntax is 'chown user:group file'. You will need sudo to change ownership of a file you do not own.

hints.title: Full solution pattern
hints.content: Run 'sudo chown intern:intern logs/app.log' to take ownership, then 'echo "Application started" >> logs/app.log' to write the log entry.
